ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for char siew soh; mapped to closest parent class 'stuffed pastry food product' (FOODON_03315091) as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for char siew soh; A0CKP (filled/savoury baked pastry) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for char siew soh or its immediate parent category in clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical char siew soh piece weighs approximately 55–65 g; a single piece (60 g) provides around 226 kcal and is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or dim sum snack.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew Soh — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database lists Char Siew Soh (F05020015) with direct per-100g nutrient values. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry. Saturated fat is estimated from the HPB total fat figure using standard lard/shortening fatty acid profiles (approximately 38% saturated of total fat), as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for char siew soh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to baked wheat-flour pastry / savoury puff pastry items (GI range 59–70 in peer-reviewed literature). The refined wheat flour shell dominates the glycemic response; the fat-rich filling moderates but does not substantially lower GI. GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (27.8 g/100g × 60 g serving = 16.68 g) / 100 = 10.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 355 mg/100g combined with high saturated fat from lard-based pastry
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit char siew soh to occasional consumption (no more than one piece) due to its 355 mg sodium per 100 g — equivalent to ~213 mg per piece — and high saturated fat content (~9.6 g/100g) which contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat pastry shell (27.8 g carbohydrate/100g, only 1.9 g fibre) with estimated medium-high GI (~65)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict char siew soh to one piece per occasion at most, pair it with a high-fibre vegetable dish to blunt postprandial glucose excursion, and avoid it entirely when blood glucose is poorly controlled, given its low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio and medium-high glycemic index.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (25.2 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~9.6 g/100g from lard or shortening, plus elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Char siew soh is unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat content (~9.6 g/100g, likely from lard or hydrogenated shortening) and elevated sodium (355 mg/100g), both of which raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; limit to rare occasional intake and choose lower-fat alternatives where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory saturated and likely trans fats from lard/shortening pastry; arachidonic acid from pork filling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly minimise char siew soh because the lard-based or shortening-based pastry is a source of saturated fat and potentially trans fats, and the pork filling contributes arachidonic acid — all of which amplify inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to RA disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siew Soh (叉烧酥 / 叉燒酥) is a baked Cantonese-style flaky pastry filled with char siew — Chinese barbecued pork seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and sesame oil. It is a staple of Singaporean and Malaysian dim sum culture and is widely sold at hawker centres, dim sum restaurants, and Chinese bakeries. The pastry shell is made from wheat flour and lard or vegetable shortening, producing a distinctively layered, crumbly, golden-brown exterior. The filling is sweet-savoury, tender, and slightly sticky. Per 100 g, char siew soh provides 376 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 25.2 g fat, and 27.8 g carbohydrate with only 1.9 g dietary fibre, making it a calorie-dense, high-fat, moderate-carbohydrate snack. Sodium content is 355 mg/100g, reflecting the soy sauce and oyster sauce in the filling. The high saturated fat content (estimated ~9.6 g/100g from lard or shortening) and elevated sodium make this food unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is commonly eaten as a breakfast item or morning tea snack, typically one to two pieces per serving. Clinically, its refined carbohydrate shell and low fibre content produce a medium-to-high glycemic response, and its fat profile raises LDL cholesterol risk with frequent consumption.

Can diabetics eat char siew soh?

What is the GI of char siew soh?
How much sodium in char siew soh?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05020015 for Char Siew Soh, which is an exact match. Saturated fat is adapted from total fat using standard lard/shortening fatty acid profiles and is documented as an estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature as no direct measurement exists.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.6 g/100g) is estimated at approximately 38% of total fat (25.2 g/100g), consistent with lard and vegetable shortening fatty acid profiles; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for char siew soh are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05020015) or in USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite pastry as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on baked wheat-flour savoury pastry items in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for char siew soh is available. GL per 60 g serving = 10.8. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional char siew soh uses pork and lard, making it non-halal by default. 5. Trans fat content is unconfirmed; if hydrogenated vegetable shortening is used in the pastry, trans fat may be present — this is a clinical caution for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease contexts.
